title: Support Vectors: The Boundary Anchors
concept: Support vectors are the critical data points that lie directly on the edges of the margin. They act as anchors that determine exactly where the boundary line is drawn; other data points can be moved or deleted without affecting the boundary at all.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the benefit of a wider margin in an SVM classification model?

options.text: It provides a larger safety buffer, making the model more accurate on new data
It allows the model to process predictions in half the time
It forces the boundary line to always be curved

exercises.explain: Spot on! A wider margin is a wider empty street, creating a safer buffer that handles new data points without classification errors.

cards.title: Fence Posts
cards.body: Imagine you are building a straight wooden fence to divide two large campsites. To make sure the fence stands straight, you only need to anchor it with sturdy posts at the very edges of the campers' spaces. Campers sleeping far back in their tents have zero impact on where the fence goes.

cards.title: What are Support Vectors?
cards.body: Support vectors are the specific data points that sit directly on the outer edges of the margin. They are the closest data points to the boundary line. They are called support vectors because they "support" or hold up the position of the boundary.

cards.title: Bridge Pillars
cards.body: Think of a suspension bridge. The heavy concrete pillars built on the riverbanks support the entire weight of the bridge. The buildings in the city miles away don't help hold the bridge up. Similarly, only the support vectors hold up the decision boundary.

cards.title: Review Sorting
cards.body: An SVM separates movie reviews into "positive" or "negative". The support vectors are the reviews right on the border — reviews that are neutral or slightly positive/negative. Extremely positive or extremely negative reviews don't affect the boundary line's position at all.

cards.title: Most Points Don't Matter
cards.body: You might think every single data point in a dataset is equally important to the model. In reality, if you delete 90% of the data points that are far away from the margin, the SVM boundary line won't budge by even a millimeter. Only the support vectors matter!

cards.title: Boundary Anchors vs. Spacers
cards.body: How data points affect the boundary line position:

left.text: Sit right on the margin's edge. Moving or removing these points will shift the entire boundary line.

right.text: Sit far away from the margin. Changing or deleting these points has zero effect on the boundary.

exercises.prompt: What are support vectors in a Support Vector Machine?

options.text: The data points closest to the margin that actively define the boundary line
The total number of arrows drawn on the final data chart
The data points that represent classification errors
The computer servers used to run the machine learning model

exercises.explain: Yes! Support vectors are the data points lying right on the curb of the margin. They are the only points that dictate where the boundary is drawn.

exercises.prompt: True or False: If you delete a data point that is sitting far away from the margin, the SVM boundary line will not move.
exercises.explain: Correct! Because they are not support vectors, points far away from the margin have no influence on the boundary line's position.

exercises.sentence: Support vectors sit directly on the outer edges of the ___.
exercises.explain: The support vectors are the boundary anchors that touch the edges of the margin (the empty buffer zone).

exercises.prompt: Which of these points is acting as a support vector in an SVM?

options.text: A point that lies directly on the margin boundary line.
A point deep inside the middle of a dense cluster of similar points.
A point that has been classified as an extreme outlier far from the line.

exercises.explain: Exactly! The points right on the margin border are the support vectors.

exercises.prompt: You have trained an SVM to classify fruits as apples or oranges. You add 500 more photos of extremely bright, obvious oranges to the dataset, all of which sit far away from the boundary line. What happens to the boundary line?

options.text: Nothing changes, because the new photos are far away from the margin and do not act as support vectors.
The boundary line shifts toward the new orange photos because there are more of them.
The boundary line turns into a circle to wrap around the new oranges.
The margin shrinks because the model becomes confused by the extra data.

exercises.explain: Fantastic! Because those new orange pictures are far away from the margin, they are not support vectors. They have zero influence on the boundary. The model only cares about the tricky points near the line!
